evals.prompt: Set up XCUITest for our SwiftUI app and write a first login test.
evals.expected_output: Should check qa-context.md or ask. Should outline: add a UI test target alongside the app target in Xcode, instantiate `XCUIApplication()`, set `continueAfterFailure = false`, pass `launchArguments`/`launchEnvironment` to flag test mode, then `app.launch()`. Provide a sample test using `accessibilityIdentifier`-based queries (`app.textFields["login.email"]`, `app.buttons["login.submit"]`). Should remind the user to set `.accessibilityIdentifier(...)` in SwiftUI view code. Should use `waitForExistence(timeout:)` for the post-login assertion. Should use synthetic credentials. Should NOT invent XCTest API.
assertions: Reads qa-context.md or asks for it

evals.prompt: Our XCUITest fails because we localize labels. Tests work in English but break in German.
evals.expected_output: Should explain that querying by `label` matches localized user-visible text. Switch to `accessibilityIdentifier` (constant across locales). Show: in the app, set `.accessibilityIdentifier("login.submit")` on the SwiftUI button; in the test, query `app.buttons["login.submit"]`. Should also mention test plans as a way to run the same suite across locales for true cross-locale coverage. Should NOT suggest hardcoding German labels in the test.
assertions: Identifies accessibilityLabel/localization as the root cause
Mentions test plans for locale matrix
Refuses hardcoded localized strings in tests

evals.prompt: how do i tap a row in a list at position 4
evals.expected_output: Should treat the casual phrasing as a valid trigger. Should show `app.cells.element(boundBy: 3).tap()` (index 3 for position 4, 0-based). Should warn that index-based queries are brittle to list order changes; recommend instead identifying by a descendant element via `app.cells.containing(.staticText, identifier: "specific-id").element.tap()` or by `accessibilityIdentifier` on the cell itself. Should not invent XCUIElementQuery methods.
assertions: Recommends descendant matching or cell identifier

evals.prompt: We need to test that the app handles a push notification arriving. The simulator never receives them.
evals.expected_output: Should explain: modern Xcode + iOS simulator supports push via `xcrun simctl push <udid> <bundle-id> payload.json` (or by drag-drop of a JSON onto the simulator). Show a minimal JSON push payload and the CLI command. Should mention that full push-from-APNs flow can only be tested on a real device with a provisioning profile (cross-reference cloud-test-grids and AWS Device Farm / BrowserStack). Should NOT pretend the simulator + production APNs setup is the same.
assertions: Names xcrun simctl push as the simulator option
Clarifies that full APNs flow requires real device

evals.prompt: Our XCUITest suite takes 45 minutes. How do we parallelize?
evals.expected_output: Should explain XCUITest parallelization: in the test plan / scheme settings, enable 'Execute in parallel' for the UI test target. This launches multiple simulator clones, each running a subset. Should mention: (1) `-parallel-testing-enabled YES -parallel-testing-worker-count <N>` on `xcodebuild`; (2) tests must be independent (no shared mutable state, no shared logged-in user that mutates between tests); (3) simulator clones have higher RAM/CPU cost — set worker count to match the host. Should mention that sharding across multiple CI machines (each running a subset) is the next step. Cross-reference ci-test-orchestration.
assertions: Notes resource cost of simulator clones
Mentions cross-machine sharding as next step

evals.prompt: Compare XCUITest with Appium for our iOS-only team.
evals.expected_output: Should frame as a trade-off. XCUITest: native Apple tool, fast iteration via Xcode, integrated with Xcode Cloud, no external server needed, faster than Appium on iOS. Appium: cross-platform infrastructure (if Android may come later), language flexibility (write iOS tests in JS/Python/Java if the team is JS/Python/Java-centric), shared cloud farm patterns. For an iOS-only Swift/Kotlin team, XCUITest is usually the better default; for a JS-only team or one that may expand to Android, Appium makes more sense. Defer to qa-context.md (single platform vs multi, team language). Cross-references appium.
assertions: Frames as trade-off, not a winner
Recommends XCUITest for iOS-only Swift teams as the default
